Q1: My study routine was honestly pretty scattered. I tried to dedicate about 18 hours a week, but with limited resources at home and high anxiety, I often found myself studying in bursts rather than steady sessions. I'm a visual learner, so I'd spend a lot of time watching lecture videos and creating diagrams, but organizing everything into a consistent schedule was really challenging for me. Some weeks I'd be super focused, other weeks I'd struggle to even start.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on deadlines and how I was feeling mentally. When assignments were due, I'd cram and log in more frequently, but during calmer weeks my attendance would drop. The stress really affected my consistency—some days my anxiety was so high I couldn't focus at all, so I'd just avoid everything until the pressure built up again. I wish I could say I had regular times, but that wasn't my reality.
Q3: Usually it was either an upcoming deadline or seeing a notification about new course content that prompted me to log in. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if there was anything I'd missed, which happened often because my attendance wasn't great. The discussion forums also pulled me in—I actually enjoyed interacting with peers there, so that was a more positive reason to log in compared to just checking requirements.
Q4: Yeah, after a few weeks, checking the LMS in the morning became kind of automatic for me. I'd wake up, make tea, and scroll through the course updates before anything else. The video lectures especially became routine since I'm a visual learner and they helped things click for me. Once I realized the discussion posts were actually helpful for understanding concepts, participating there became part of my normal week too.
Q5: The video lectures were absolutely the most useful for me—I'd watch them multiple times, sometimes at different speeds. Being a visual learner, seeing concepts explained with diagrams and demonstrations made everything stick better than reading text. The discussion forums were also valuable because I could see how other students interpreted things and that helped fill gaps in my understanding. Peer interactions there gave me perspectives I wouldn't have found on my own.
Q6: I often ignored the reading materials and some of the supplementary PDFs. With limited resources, I couldn't always print things out, and reading long documents on my phone was exhausting. Some optional practice quizzes I also skipped because they felt overwhelming when I was already stressed about the main assignments. If something didn't seem directly connected to my grade, my low motivation made it easy to push aside.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see immediate relevance to an assignment or exam. Visual content like videos or interactive diagrams always felt valuable because they matched how I learn best. If something helped reduce my anxiety about an upcoming deadline, that also made it feel worthwhile. Honestly, with my stress levels being so high, I had to be selective about where I put my energy.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't always afford mentally. Required ones came with this pressure and anxiety that forced me to engage, even when I didn't want to. Optional stuff, even if interesting, often got pushed aside because I was already struggling to keep up with the mandatory work. Sometimes I'd feel guilty about skipping optional content, but my energy was just too limited.
Q9: Definitely. I downloaded a lot of videos and PDFs to study offline because my internet isn't always reliable. I'd take handwritten notes from the videos and review those instead of rewatching online. I also used some free YouTube tutorials and external resources when the course materials weren't clicking for me. A lot of my actual learning happened with just my notebook and downloaded files, away from the LMS entirely.
Q10: There were many times I'd be studying intensely from my handwritten notes or downloaded materials, but the LMS wouldn't show any activity. I'd spend hours working through problems on paper or creating visual mind maps from memory. Sometimes I'd discuss concepts with a friend over the phone, which helped me understand things better but obviously left no digital trace. My offline study time probably exceeded my online time.
Q11: Yes, this was pretty common for me. I'd download a video or PDF once, then study from it repeatedly without going back online. With my internet being unreliable and resources being limited, it made more sense to grab everything I needed in one session. I'd annotate PDFs on my phone or take notes by hand, then review those notes multiple times throughout the week without touching the LMS again.
Q12: There were definitely periods when my anxiety spiked and engaging with anything felt impossible. Around midterms, I had this overwhelming feeling that I was falling behind, which made it even harder to start. Sometimes competing responsibilities at home pulled my attention away, and the low motivation made it hard to push through. When multiple deadlines clustered together, I'd almost shut down from the stress.
Q13: Absolutely. When I was fatigued or overwhelmed, I'd log into the LMS but just scroll without really absorbing anything. Confusion about certain topics made me avoid those modules entirely, which hurt my assignment completion rate. Competing responsibilities meant I sometimes had to choose between studying and other obligations, and the course often lost. My high stress levels definitely showed up in how inconsistently I used the platform.
Q14: Yeah, there were times I'd have a video playing but my mind was completely elsewhere, worrying about other things. I'd scroll through discussion posts without really reading them, just going through the motions. Sometimes I'd open an assignment, stare at it for twenty minutes, and close it without making any progress. Those moments felt like wasted time, but I couldn't seem to snap out of it.
Q15: I postponed assignments more than I should have, even knowing they were important. The anxiety made starting feel overwhelming—like once I began, I'd have to face how much I didn't understand. Sometimes I'd convince myself I'd do it tomorrow when I felt better, but tomorrow I'd feel the same. It was this cycle of avoidance that hurt my assignment completion rate, even though I knew better.
Q16: Deadlines basically controlled my LMS usage. I'd be relatively inactive until a deadline approached, then I'd log in frantically, watch videos at double speed, and try to catch up. The pressure of deadlines was the main thing that cut through my low motivation and forced action. It wasn't healthy, but that external pressure was often the only thing that got me moving when my internal motivation failed.
Q17: When I got positive feedback, it gave me a small boost and made me want to engage more. But negative or critical feedback sometimes increased my anxiety and made me want to avoid the course entirely for a few days. I appreciated detailed feedback because it helped me understand what to fix, but emotionally processing it was hard. The timing mattered too—feedback right before a new deadline felt overwhelming.
Q18: The course structure with its regular video releases actually helped me because I could plan my visual learning around them. But having twenty online courses meant the structure sometimes felt like too much to track. When modules built on each other, missing one made everything after feel impossible to catch up on. The weekly rhythm helped somewhat, but I often fell out of sync with it.
Q19: The video content made engagement easier since it matched my learning style perfectly. The discussion forums being active also helped because peer interaction kept me connected to the course. What made it harder was when assignments required resources I didn't have easy access to, or when instructions weren't visually clear. Dense text-based content was always a barrier for me compared to visual explanations.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is trying to understand material but struggling to produce work that meets the standard. Maybe they're watching videos repeatedly, participating in discussions, trying to learn—but when it comes to assignments, anxiety or confusion blocks them. It could also be someone dealing with personal issues that make completing work hard even when they're genuinely trying to engage with the content.
Q21: That actually resonates with me somewhat. I did a lot of learning offline—from downloaded materials, handwritten notes, external resources. My exam score was decent even though my LMS activity and assignment completion weren't great. Someone might understand the material well through independent study but not show high platform engagement. The LMS doesn't capture everything about how someone actually learns.
Q22: I think course logs can accurately show login patterns, time spent on videos, and participation in discussions—the quantifiable stuff. But they miss the quality of engagement, like whether someone was actually focused or just had a tab open. They can't capture offline study, emotional state, or the depth of understanding someone develops. A lot of real learning happens in ways that don't leave digital footprints.
Q23: To me, being engaged means actually thinking about the material, not just going through motions. It's when I'm making connections, asking myself questions, and trying to apply concepts. It's not just about logging in or watching videos—it's about whether my brain is actively working on understanding. Real engagement for me happens when I'm drawing diagrams, discussing with peers, or wrestling with a problem even when it's frustrating.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I struggled with anxiety before even opening the platform, or the hours I spent studying from handwritten notes and downloaded files. It wouldn't capture the peer conversations that helped me understand concepts, or the mindfulness exercises I did to manage stress enough to study. My emotional journey through the course—the fear, the small victories, the overwhelm—none of that shows up in login data.
